comments.body: What do you want hibernate to do when you read/update that entity?

Why can't you just have a DTO with 2 fields of the Date entity - date referring to the original entity you select in your query and replacementBlock as the entity you seem to be joining to the first one? This would even allow you to get rid of the native query and use JPAs DTO projection mechanism ("SELECT new org.foo.DateWithReplacement(sch, replacementBlock) FROM Date sch ....")

Best practice is.. to use the JPA entity annotations as they are. You have a data value that is in another table, so presumably it already has entity representation somewhere, and should/could have mapped the sch.id = block.templateappointmentid relationship in the model somehow. So what you are asking for is a projection of these two entities which avoids you having to forever modify the existing GetDatesJpaEntity entity to handle non-existing fields. Treat it like an entity or a DTO, but not both if you can. Otherwise, map it to the entity as a secondary table and not ever insert the entity

title: How to map additional aliased columns from a native query to a JPA entity when the column does not physically exist?
body:
I have the following Spring Data JPA repository method using a native query with an aliased column that does not exist physically in the database table:

@Repository
public interface GetDatesByIdRepository extends JpaRepository<GetDatesJpaEntity, Long> {
    @Query(
        value = """
            select
                sch.id,
                sch.status,
                sch.patientid,
                sch.appointmentdate,
                sch.starttime,
                sch.duration,
                sch.departmentid,
                sch.templatedId,
                block.id as replacementblockId
            from
                Dates sch
            left join Dates block on sch.id = block.templateappointmentid and sch.id != block.id
            where
                sch.id = :dateId
            """,
        nativeQuery = true)
    GetDatesJpaEntity findDatesByIdWithReplacement(Long dateId);
}

And the entity:

@AllArgsConstructor
@NoArgsConstructor
@Entity
public class GetDatesJpaEntity {
    @Id
    @Column(name = "ID")
    public long id;

    @Column(name = "TEMPLATEDID")
    public Long templatedId;

    @Column(name = "PATIENTLOCATIONID")
    public Long patientLocationId;

    @Column(name = "PATIENTID")
    public Long patientId;

    @Column(name = "STATUS")
    public String status;

    @Column(name = "APPOINTMENTDATE")
    public LocalDateTime appointmentDate;

    @Column(name = "STARTTIME")
    public LocalDateTime startTime;

    @Column(name = "DURATION")
    public Long duration;

    @Column(name = "DEPARTMENTID")
    public Long departmentId;
}

The problem is that the aliased column replacementblockId does not exist in the table and is not mapped in the entity. I initially tried to add this field to the entity as:

@Column(name = "REPLACEMENTBLOCKID")
private long replacementBlockId;

But this causes errors because the column does not physically exist.

What is the best practice or recommended approach to handle this scenario in Spring Data JPA? I want to avoid major refactoring such as introducing DTOs if possible.
